page: Amazon Web Services m2.xlarge is a second-generation general-purpose instance type built on the Xen hypervisor. It features an x86_64 architecture powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2665 0 processor running at 2.4 GHz. The instance is configured with 2 dedicated physical cores and 17.0 GB of system memory, yielding a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.55 GB per core. Local storage is provided via a bundled 420 GB HDD, while network baseline bandwidth is rated at 0 Gbps.

Benchmark evaluations place the m2.xlarge in the lower performance tiers. The single-core stress-ng CPU performance score of 1310.39 is categorized as weak, while the multi-core score of 1719.98 falls into the bottom 10% of tested servers. Memory bandwidth is similarly constrained, with read-only large block transfers reaching 13.55 GB/sec and write large block transfers at 11.48 GB/sec. Cryptographic and vector processing capabilities are limited due to the absence of modern instruction sets such as AVX, AVX-512, and AES-NI.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the m2.xlarge offers a high memory capacity relative to its compute resources, but its legacy hardware architecture presents significant performance tradeoffs. The reliance on HDD local storage and the lack of baseline network bandwidth restrict its suitability for modern high-throughput applications. This instance is best suited for legacy workloads, basic database operations, or lightweight general-purpose tasks that require a larger memory footprint than standard entry-level instances but do not demand high CPU or network performance.
description: Amazon Web Services m2.xlarge is a legacy general-purpose instance featuring 2 dedicated Intel Xeon E5-2665 0 cores running at 2.4 GHz on an x86_64 architecture. It is equipped with 17.0 GB of RAM, providing a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.55 GB per core, and includes 420 GB of local HDD storage. Operating on the Xen hypervisor, the instance lacks modern CPU instruction sets like AVX and AES-NI, resulting in poor to weak benchmark performance across compute and memory bandwidth tests. With a baseline network bandwidth of 0 Gbps, the m2.xlarge is best suited for legacy applications, basic database operations, and memory-dependent workloads that do not require high computational throughput or fast network connectivity.
og_description: Amazon Web Services m2.xlarge is a legacy general-purpose instance with 2 Intel Xeon cores, 17.0 GB of RAM, and a 420 GB local HDD, suitable for memory-dependent legacy workloads.
